summary: Gruber discusses the COVID-19 crisis exposing how critical U.S. infrastructure still runs on COBOL legacy systems, with states scrambling to find programmers fluent in the decades-old language. He then pivots to a linguistic observation about how 'coding' has replaced 'programming' in common usage, arguing the shift reflects a cultural devaluation of the craft. He contends that 'programming' carries weight and seriousness that 'coding' lacks, comparing the distinction to 'writing' versus 'jotting.' He concludes that the explosive growth in demand has flooded the field with people who code but can't truly program.
key_points: At least 12 U.S. states still run unemployment systems on COBOL, and the shortage of COBOL programmers has become a crisis during COVID-19
Almost all popular modern languages are fundamentally C derivatives and mutually intelligible, but COBOL is from another universe entirely
The shift from 'programming' to 'coding' in common parlance reflects a cultural move from seriousness to informality, reversing the '90s trend toward 'software engineering'
The explosive growth in demand has brought many people into the field who 'couldn't program their way out of a paper bag'
Programming is an art and code is merely its medium — the linguistic shift obscures this distinction
money_quotes: Programming is a serious endeavor; coding sounds like a lark — like the difference between writing and jotting.
The Art of Computer Coding or The C Coding Language just wouldn't carry the same literary heft.
Code is merely a means to an end. Programming is an art and code is merely its medium.
Pointing a camera at a subject does not make one a proper photographer. There are a lot of self-described coders out there who couldn't program their way out of a paper bag.
Coding sounds informal, guileless, ad hoc; engineering sounds rigorous, regulated, and let's face it, pretentious.
COBOL, on the other hand, is like something from another universe.
key_insight: The linguistic shift from 'programming' to 'coding' reflects a broader cultural devaluation of software as a serious craft, even as aging infrastructure proves how much rigorous programming expertise still matters.
